vocabulary.explanation: An idiomatic expression meaning a sudden loss of nerve or confidence, especially before a major commitment like marriage. In the context of this film, one or both partners may experience 'cold feet' as their wedding week spirals into chaos.

A colloquial phrase meaning something has gone badly wrong or out of control. It appears directly in the film's synopsis ('sends their wedding week off the rails'), signaling that plans collapse in a dramatic and likely comedic fashion.

A comedic style in which humor is delivered with a completely expressionless, unemotional face or tone. Director Kristoffer Borgli is known for his deadpan approach, meaning jokes in this film may be subtle and dry rather than loud or slapstick.

An artistic style that blends realistic depictions with dreamlike, bizarre, or illogical elements. Borgli's films often carry a surrealist quality — expect moments in The Drama that feel strange or exaggerated beyond realistic logic, used to amplify emotional or comedic effect.

An industry term for films that have the artistic ambition and credibility of independent cinema but feature star-level talent and wider distribution. All three leads — Zendaya, Robert Pattinson, and Alana Haim — are associated with prestige indie projects, setting expectations for the film's tone.

A Norwegian writer-director known for Sick of Myself (2022) and Dream Scenario (2023). His work satirizes modern anxieties around identity and attention-seeking. The Drama marks his continued collaboration with Hollywood talent after his English-language debut.

An American actress and musician, one-third of the Grammy-nominated rock band HAIM. She made her acting breakthrough in Paul Thomas Anderson's Licorice Pizza (2021) and is a rising star in prestige cinema. Her casting signals the film's indie-leaning sensibility.

While 'cold feet' is a normal, temporary pre-wedding anxiety, 'commitment phobia' is a deeper psychological reluctance to enter long-term relationships. Films in this genre often explore the line between the two — and The Drama appears poised to examine what truly holds a couple together when everything goes wrong.

background.summary: The Drama (2026) is a comedy-drama-romance directed by Norwegian filmmaker Kristoffer Borgli. A happily engaged couple faces a series of chaotic and unexpected events during their wedding week, putting their relationship — and perhaps their sanity — to the ultimate test. Starring Zendaya and Robert Pattinson as the couple, with Alana Haim in a supporting role, the film blends sharp humor with emotional depth, exploring themes of commitment, identity, and the absurdity of modern relationships under pressure.
context: Wedding week chaos is a well-worn romantic comedy trope, but Borgli's signature surrealist lens is expected to give it a darkly comedic, offbeat twist that sets it apart from conventional rom-coms.

The film reunites the world of prestige indie cinema with mainstream Hollywood stars — Zendaya (Euphoria, Dune, Challengers) and Robert Pattinson (The Batman, The Lighthouse, Good Time) are both known for choosing unconventional, artistically ambitious projects.

Alana Haim, one-third of the rock band HAIM, broke into acting with her acclaimed lead role in Paul Thomas Anderson's Licorice Pizza (2021), and has since become a rising presence in indie and prestige cinema.

Themes of romantic commitment and cold feet are central — audiences can expect the film to probe questions like: What does it really mean to say 'I do'? How well do we truly know our partners? And can love survive chaos?

The film's genre blend (comedy + drama + romance) signals tonal complexity — expect moments of laugh-out-loud absurdity alongside genuine emotional vulnerability, a hallmark of Borgli's previous work.
background.director_note: Kristoffer Borgli is a Norwegian writer-director celebrated for his uniquely deadpan, satirical, and surrealist style. He first gained international attention with Sick of Myself (2022), a pitch-black Norwegian comedy about a woman who fabricates a rare illness to steal the spotlight from her artist boyfriend — a sharp skewering of narcissism and social media culture. He followed this with Dream Scenario (2023), his English-language debut starring Nicolas Cage as a mild-mannered professor who inexplicably begins appearing in strangers' dreams, only for fame to curdle into nightmare. Both films showcase Borgli's signature approach: grounding absurd, high-concept premises in painfully recognizable human psychology, using dark comedy to dissect vanity, desire for attention, and the fragility of identity. His filmmaking style is precise and restrained — he favors a cool, observational camera, dry wit, and a slow-burn escalation of chaos. With The Drama, Borgli brings this sensibility to the romantic comedy genre, suggesting viewers can expect something far more psychologically layered and wickedly funny than a standard wedding film.
